devanagari: बल्वलवधः सूतहत्यामार्जनाय बलभद्रस्य तीर्थेषु भ्रमणं च । अथैकोनाशीतितमोऽध्यायः । श्रीशुक उवाच । ततः पर्वण्युपावृत्ते प्रचण्डः पांशुवर्षण...
english_devnagari: balvalavadhaḥ sūtahatyāmārjanāya balabhadrasya tīrtheṣu bhramaṇaṃ caathaikonāśītitamo'dhyāyaḥśrīśuka uvācatataḥ parvaṇyupāvṛtte pracaṇḍaḥ pāṃśuvarṣaṇaḥbhīmo vāyurabhūdrājanpūyagandhastu sarvaśaḥ
translation:
Śrī Śuka narrated:
O king Parīkṣit! When the next parva (full moon) day came, a terrible, violent stormy wind swept over the place showering heavy dust and filling the whole atmosphere with obnoxious odour on all sides.

devanagari: ततोऽमेध्यमयं वर्षं बल्वलेन विनिर्मितम् । अभवद्यज्ञशालायां सोऽन्वदृश्यत शूलधृक् ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: tato'medhyamayaṃ varṣaṃ balvalena vinirmitamabhavadyajñaśālāyāṃ so'nvadṛśyata śūladhṛk
translation: Then there was a downpour of filthy things on the sacrificial ground, caused by Balvala, after which he appeared with a trident in hand.

devanagari: तं विलोक्य बृहत्कायं भिन्नाञ्जनचयोपमम् । तप्तताम्रशिखाश्मश्रुं दंष्ट्रोग्रभ्रुकुटीमुखम् ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: taṃ vilokya bṛhatkāyaṃ bhinnāñjanacayopamamtaptatāmraśikhāśmaśruṃ daṃṣṭrograbhrukuṭīmukham
translation: Beholding that asura of gigantic body, extremely black like a heap of collyrium, with hair on the head, moustaches and beard like red-hot copper and a face terror-inspiring with its fierce tusks and terrific knit brows, Balarāma remembered (his weapons)—the pestle that shatters hostile forces and the plough which vanquishes demons. And both the weapons immediately presented themselves to him.

devanagari: सस्मार मूषलं रामः परसैन्यविदारणम् । हलं च दैत्यदमनं ते तूर्णमुपतस्थतुः ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: sasmāra mūṣalaṃ rāmaḥ parasainyavidāraṇamhalaṃ ca daityadamanaṃ te tūrṇamupatasthatuḥ
translation: Beholding that asura of gigantic body, extremely black like a heap of collyrium, with hair on the head, moustaches and beard like red-hot copper and a face terror-inspiring with its fierce tusks and terrific knit brows, Balarāma remembered (his weapons)—the pestle that shatters hostile forces and the plough which vanquishes demons. And both the weapons immediately presented themselves to him.

devanagari: तमाकृष्य हलाग्रेण बल्वलं गगनेचरम् । मूषलेनाहनत्क्रुद्धो मूर्ध्नि ब्रह्मद्रुहं बलः ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: tamākṛṣya halāgreṇa balvalaṃ gaganecarammūṣalenāhanatkruddho mūrdhni brahmadruhaṃ balaḥ
translation: With the point of his plough, he pulled to the ground, Balvala who was ranging through the sky and full of indignation, he struck that sworn enemy of Brāhmaṇas on the head with a pestle.

devanagari: सोऽपतद्भुवि निर्भिन्न ललाटोऽसृक्समुत्सृजन् । मुञ्चन्नार्तस्वरं शैलो यथा वज्रहतोऽरुणः ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: so'patadbhuvi nirbhinna lalāṭo'sṛksamutsṛjanmuñcannārtasvaraṃ śailo yathā vajrahato'ruṇaḥ
translation: With his forehead shattered and split open and bleeding profusely, the demon gave out a helpless yell and fell down to the ground like a mountain struck with Vajra and covered with liquid red chalk.

devanagari: संस्तुत्य मुनयो रामं प्रयुज्यावितथाशिषः । अभ्यषिञ्चन्महाभागा वृत्रघ्नं विबुधा यथा ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: saṃstutya munayo rāmaṃ prayujyāvitathāśiṣaḥabhyaṣiñcanmahābhāgā vṛtraghnaṃ vibudhā yathā
translation: The sages applauded Balarāma (for this feat) and pronounced their unfailing benedictions. Those highly blessed sages sprinkled sacred waters on Balarāma (as abhiṣeka) as gods did to Indra when he killed Vṛtra.

devanagari: वैजयन्तीं ददुर्मालां श्रीधामाम्लानपङ्कजां । रामाय वाससी दिव्ये दिव्यान्याभरणानि च ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: vaijayantīṃ dadurmālāṃ śrīdhāmāmlānapaṅkajāṃrāmāya vāsasī divye divyānyābharaṇāni ca
translation: They presented to Balarāma a celestial five-coloured Vaijayanti garland of unfading flowers (chiefly lotuses, two excellent pieces of cloth and very valuable ornaments.

devanagari: अथ तैरभ्यनुज्ञातः कौशिकीमेत्य ब्राह्मणैः । स्नात्वा सरोवरमगाद्यतः सरयूरास्रवत् ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: atha tairabhyanujñātaḥ kauśikīmetya brāhmaṇaiḥsnātvā sarovaramagādyataḥ sarayūrāsravat
translation: Then with the permission of those sages, Balarāma, accompanied with Brāhmaṇas went to the Kauśikī[1] river. After taking bath therein, he proceeded to the lake[2] from which the Sarayū flows out.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
Modern Kośi running from Nepal by the western border of the Purnea district in Bihar. The river has been tamed by dams now. Was it in the Kokāmukha tīrtha where Balarāma took his bath?—SGAMI, pp. 278-80, 340.

Footnote [2]:
The Purāṇas believe that the Mānasa lake is the source of the Sarayū. But the river rises in the mountains of Kumaun and after its junction with the Kālīnadī, it is called the Sarayū. Ayodhyā is on its bank.—GDAMI, p. 181-82.

devanagari: अनुस्रोतेन सरयूं प्रयागमुपगम्य सः । स्नात्वा सन्तर्प्य देवादीन्जगाम पुलहाश्रमम् ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: anusrotena sarayūṃ prayāgamupagamya saḥsnātvā santarpya devādīnjagāma pulahāśramam
translation: From its source and travelling along the current of the Sarayū, Balarāma reached Prayāga. He took his bath there in, offered water oblations to gods (sages and manes) and proceeded to the hermitage of Pulaha.[3]
purport:
Footnote [3]:
The same as Śālagrāma, near the source of the Gandak—GDAMI, p. 161 & 174.

devanagari: गोमतीं गण्डकीं स्नात्वा विपाशां शोण आप्लुतः । गयां गत्वा पितॄनिष्ट्वा गङ्गासागरसङ्गमे ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: gomatīṃ gaṇḍakīṃ snātvā vipāśāṃ śoṇa āplutaḥgayāṃ gatvā pitṝniṣṭvā gaṅgāsāgarasaṅgame
translation: He bathed in the Gomatī, Gaṇḍakī, Vipāśā[4] the Śoṇa and visited Gayā and worshipped his ancestors. After taking his bath at the confluence of the Gaṅgā with the sea, he met Paraśurāma and paid his respects to him on the Mahendra[5] hill. He took bath in the seven branches of the Godāvarī, the Veṇā, the Pampā[6] and the river Bhīmā.
purport:
Footnote [4]:
The Beas. It is difficult to plot the route followed by Balarāma as the purāṇa writer has vague idea of the geography of north India.

Footnote [5]:
The range of hills extending from Orissa to the district of Madura. It is applied to Eastern Ghats in general—SGAMI, p. 61. N.L. De states that Mahendra hills separate Ganjam from the valley of the Mahanadi—GDAMI p. 119.

Footnote [6]:
A tributary of the Tuṅgabhadrā. It rises in the Rsvamuka hills in Bellary district.

devanagari: उपस्पृश्य महेन्द्रा द्रौ रामं दृष्ट्वाभिवाद्य च । सप्तगोदावरीं वेणां पम्पां भीमरथीं ततः ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: upaspṛśya mahendrā drau rāmaṃ dṛṣṭvābhivādya casaptagodāvarīṃ veṇāṃ pampāṃ bhīmarathīṃ tataḥ
translation: He bathed in the Gomatī, Gaṇḍakī, Vipāśā[4] the Śoṇa and visited Gayā and worshipped his ancestors. After taking his bath at the confluence of the Gaṅgā with the sea, he met Paraśurāma and paid his respects to him on the Mahendra[5] hill. He took bath in the seven branches of the Godāvarī, the Veṇā, the Pampā[6] and the river Bhīmā.
purport:
Footnote [4]:
The Beas. It is difficult to plot the route followed by Balarāma as the purāṇa writer has vague idea of the geography of north India.

Footnote [5]:
The range of hills extending from Orissa to the district of Madura. It is applied to Eastern Ghats in general—SGAMI, p. 61. N.L. De states that Mahendra hills separate Ganjam from the valley of the Mahanadi—GDAMI p. 119.

Footnote [6]:
A tributary of the Tuṅgabhadrā. It rises in the Rsvamuka hills in Bellary district.

devanagari: स्कन्दं दृष्ट्वा ययौ रामः श्रीशैलं गिरिशालयम् । द्र विडेषु महापुण्यं दृष्ट्वाद्रिं वेङ्कटं प्रभुः ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: skandaṃ dṛṣṭvā yayau rāmaḥ śrīśailaṃ giriśālayamdra viḍeṣu mahāpuṇyaṃ dṛṣṭvādriṃ veṅkaṭaṃ prabhuḥ
translation: After paying a visit to the temple of Skanda, he went to Śrī Śaila[7]. The great Balarāma then visited the holy mountain Veṅkaṭa in the Draviḍa land.
purport:
Footnote [7]:
Also called Śrī Parvata (GDAMI, p. 193) It is a hili in the Nallanialur range and a famous Tirtha in Kurnool district of Andhra Pradesh.—SGAMI, p. 336.

devanagari: कामकोष्णीं पुरीं काञ्चीं कावेरीं च सरिद्वराम् । श्रीरन्गाख्यं महापुण्यं यत्र सन्निहितो हरिः ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: kāmakoṣṇīṃ purīṃ kāñcīṃ kāverīṃ ca saridvarāmśrīrangākhyaṃ mahāpuṇyaṃ yatra sannihito hariḥ
translation: Lord Balarāma then visited the city of Kāñcī[8] known as Kāmakoṣṇī[9] (Kāmakoṭī) and the great river Kāverī, he paid visit to the holy place known as Śrīraṅga[10] (Srirangam) where Lord Hari ever abides.
purport:
Footnote [8]:
Modern Conjeveram on the river Palar, 43 miles south-west of Madras. The eastern part of the town is called Viṣṇu Kāñcī and western part as Śiva kāñcī—GDAMI, p. 86

Footnote [9]:
N.L. De identifies it with Kumbhakonam but he is doubtful about it.—GDAMI, p. 86.

Footnote [10]:
Śrīraṅgam, 2 miles to the north of Trichinopoly, Tamil Nad. Famous for its Viṣṇu temple (Śrī Raṅgam)—GDAMI, p. 193.

devanagari: ऋषभाद्रिं हरेः क्षेत्रं दक्षिणां मथुरां तथा । सामुद्रं सेतुमगमत्महापातकनाशनम् ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: ṛṣabhādriṃ hareḥ kṣetraṃ dakṣiṇāṃ mathurāṃ tathāsāmudraṃ setumagamatmahāpātakanāśanam
translation: He then went to the Ṛṣabha[11] hill, a sacred place dedicated to Hari. He then visited Setu[12]—the dam built across the sea (by Rāmacandra) where people are absolved of very heinous sins.
purport:
Footnote [11]:
The Palni hills in Madura. They form the northern portion of the Malaya mountain. The hills are locally known as Varāha Parvata—GDAMI, p. 169.

Footnote [12]:
The Adam’s bridge between India and Ceylon Rameshwaram is the first island in this chain of islets. It is called Śaṅgama Tīrtha also—GDAMI, p. 184.

devanagari: तत्रायुतमदाद्धेनूर्ब्राह्मणेभ्यो हलायुधः । कृतमालां ताम्रपर्णीं मलयं च कुलाचलम् ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: tatrāyutamadāddhenūrbrāhmaṇebhyo halāyudhaḥkṛtamālāṃ tāmraparṇīṃ malayaṃ ca kulācalam
translation: Balarāma gave ten thousand cows to the Brāhmaṇas there. He then visited the rivers Kṛta-mālā, Tāmraparṇī and the mountain-chain called the Malaya (one of the seven important mountain ranges in India).

devanagari: तत्रागस्त्यं समासीनं नमस्कृत्याभिवाद्य च । योजितस्तेन चाशीर्भिरनुज्ञातो गतोऽर्णवम् ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: tatrāgastyaṃ samāsīnaṃ namaskṛtyābhivādya cayojitastena cāśīrbhiranujñāto gato'rṇavam
translation: There he paid respects to the sage Agastya who was established on the mountain. After receiving blessings from him and with his permission, Balarāma went to the south-sea where he visited the goddess Durgā (Pārvatī), known there as Kanyā (at Kanyā Kumārī).

devanagari: दक्षिणं तत्र कन्याख्यां दुर्गां देवीं ददर्श सः । ततः फाल्गुनमासाद्य पञ्चाप्सरसमुत्तमम् । विष्णुः सन्निहितो यत्र स्नात्वास्पर्शद्गवायुत...
english_devnagari: dakṣiṇaṃ tatra kanyākhyāṃ durgāṃ devīṃ dadarśa saḥtataḥ phālgunamāsādya pañcāpsarasamuttamamviṣṇuḥ sannihito yatra snātvāsparśadgavāyutam
translation: Next he visited the sacred place called Phālguna[13] and the lake called Pañcāpsaras (five celestial damsels) noted for the special presence of Viṣṇu. He performed ablution therein and gave ten thousand cows (to Brāhmaṇas).
purport:
Footnote [13]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā identifies it with Anantapun fifty-six miles to the south-east of Bellary. Pañcāpsarā tīrtha is near it. Subodhinī calls it ‘Anantaśayana’. He states that the five divine damsels were restored to their original status by Arjuna. Hence it is called Pañcāpsaras.

devanagari: ततोऽभिव्रज्य भगवान्केरलांस्तु त्रिगर्तकान् । गोकर्णाख्यं शिवक्षेत्रं सान्निध्यं यत्र धूर्जटेः ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: tato'bhivrajya bhagavānkeralāṃstu trigartakāngokarṇākhyaṃ śivakṣetraṃ sānnidhyaṃ yatra dhūrjaṭeḥ
translation: Then Lord Balarāma went to Kerala, Trigarta.[14] (From thence he proceeded to the famous Śaivite holy place Gokarṇa where god Śiva is ever-present.
purport:
Footnote [14]:
North Canara in Karnataka—GDAMI, p. 205.

devanagari: आर्यां द्वैपायनीं दृष्ट्वा शूर्पारकमगाद्बलः । तापीं पयोष्णीं निर्विन्ध्यामुपस्पृश्याथ दण्डकम् ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: āryāṃ dvaipāyanīṃ dṛṣṭvā śūrpārakamagādbalaḥtāpīṃ payoṣṇīṃ nirvindhyāmupaspṛśyātha daṇḍakam
translation: Having visited the shrine of Pārvatī (designated as Āryā) on the island, Balarāma proceeded to Śūrpāraka (Sopara, Dist. Thana, Maharashtra). After taking bath in the Tāpī, the Payoṣṇī[15] and the Nirvindhyā[16], he entered the Daṇḍaka forest.
purport:
Footnote [15]:
The Pain-gaṅgā. a tributary of the Godavari—SGAMI, p. 57.

Footnote [16]:
The Newuj, a tributary of the Chambal flowing between the Betvva and the Kalisindh—SGAMI, p. 57.

devanagari: प्रविश्य रेवामगमद्यत्र माहिष्मती पुरी । मनुतीर्थमुपस्पृश्य प्रभासं पुनरागमत् ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: praviśya revāmagamadyatra māhiṣmatī purīmanutīrthamupaspṛśya prabhāsaṃ punarāgamat
translation: (From that forest) he entered the river Narmadā on the banks of which is situated the city of Māhiṣmatī[17]. He performed ablution in the Manutīrtha and again returned to Prabhāsa.
purport:
Footnote [17]:
Modern Maheshwar, forty miles to the south of Indore. It was the capital of Haihayas. In the Buddhist canon in the Mahā Govinda sulta (Dīgha Nikāya 29.36) it is called Māhissatī and it was the capital of Avanti (Malwa).

devanagari: श्रुत्वा द्विजैः कथ्यमानं कुरुपाण्डवसंयुगे । सर्वराजन्यनिधनं भारं मेने हृतं भुवः ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: śrutvā dvijaiḥ kathyamānaṃ kurupāṇḍavasaṃyugesarvarājanyanidhanaṃ bhāraṃ mene hṛtaṃ bhuvaḥ
translation: Having heard from the Brāhmaṇas the report of the destruction of all Kṣattriya princes (participating) in the war between Kauravas and Pāṇḍavas, he considered it as the removal of the burden of the earth.

devanagari: स भीमदुर्योधनयोर्गदाभ्यां युध्यतोर्मृधे । वारयिष्यन्विनशनं जगाम यदुनन्दनः ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: sa bhīmaduryodhanayorgadābhyāṃ yudhyatormṛdhevārayiṣyanvinaśanaṃ jagāma yadunandanaḥ
translation: The scion (or the delight) of the Yadu’s race (Balarāma) went to Kurukṣetra with the desire of preventing Bhīma and Duryodhana from their single combat with their maces on the battle-field.

devanagari: युधिष्ठिरस्तु तं दृष्ट्वा यमौ कृष्णार्जुनावपि । अभिवाद्याभवंस्तुष्णीं किं विवक्षुरिहागतः ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: yudhiṣṭhirastu taṃ dṛṣṭvā yamau kṛṣṇārjunāvapiabhivādyābhavaṃstuṣṇīṃ kiṃ vivakṣurihāgataḥ
translation: As soon as Yudhiṣṭhira, the twins Nakula, Sahadeva and even Kṛṣṇa and Arjuna saw him, they bowed to him but they kept quiet, as they were afraid why he had come over there and what he wanted to say.

devanagari: गदापाणी उभौ दृष्ट्वा संरब्धौ विजयैषिणौ । मण्डलानि विचित्राणि चरन्ताविदमब्रवीत् ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: gadāpāṇī ubhau dṛṣṭvā saṃrabdhau vijayaiṣiṇaumaṇḍalāni vicitrāṇi carantāvidamabravīt
translation: He saw both Bhīma and Duryodhana, with maces in hand, both highly enraged and bent on vanquishing the other, were making wonderful manoeuvres (in the combat with maces). He spoke to them:

devanagari: युवां तुल्यबलौ वीरौ हे राजन्हे वृकोदर । एकं प्राणाधिकं मन्ये उतैकं शिक्षयाधिकम् ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: yuvāṃ tulyabalau vīrau he rājanhe vṛkodaraekaṃ prāṇādhikaṃ manye utaikaṃ śikṣayādhikam
translation: “O king (Duryodhana), O Bhīma, both of you are warriors equal in strength. I think one of you is superior in physical strength while the other excels in training.

devanagari: तस्मादेकतरस्येह युवयोः समवीर्ययोः । न लक्ष्यते जयोऽन्यो वा विरमत्वफलो रणः ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: tasmādekatarasyeha yuvayoḥ samavīryayoḥna lakṣyate jayo'nyo vā viramatvaphalo raṇaḥ
translation: As both of you are equal in prowess, I do not see that either of you will be victorious or vanquished. Hence, such a fruitless combat should be stopped altogether.

devanagari: न तद्वाक्यं जगृहतुर्बद्धवैरौ नृपार्थवत् । अनुस्मरन्तावन्योन्यं दुरुक्तं दुष्कृतानि च ॥ २८ ॥
english_devnagari: na tadvākyaṃ jagṛhaturbaddhavairau nṛpārthavatanusmarantāvanyonyaṃ duruktaṃ duṣkṛtāni ca
translation: O Parīkṣit! The enmity of these two inveterate adversaries was so deep-seated that neither of them accepted his advice, though it was salutary and significant. For they did not forget their mutual offensive words and the wicked deeds.

devanagari: दिष्टं तदनुमन्वानो रामो द्वारवतीं ययौ । उग्रसेनादिभिः प्रीतैर्ज्ञातिभिः समुपागतः ॥ २९ ॥
english_devnagari: diṣṭaṃ tadanumanvāno rāmo dvāravatīṃ yayauugrasenādibhiḥ prītairjñātibhiḥ samupāgataḥ
translation: Considering that the combat was destined (as a fruit of their past acts and hence inevitable), Balarāma returned to Dvārakā. He was received there warmly by Ugrasena and other relatives who were glad at his arrival.

devanagari: तं पुनर्नैमिषं प्राप्तमृषयोऽयाजयन्मुदा । क्रत्वङ्गं क्रतुभिः सर्वैर्निवृत्ताखिलविग्रहम् ॥ ३० ॥
english_devnagari: taṃ punarnaimiṣaṃ prāptamṛṣayo'yājayanmudākratvaṅgaṃ kratubhiḥ sarvairnivṛttākhilavigraham
translation: (Balarāma took him with all the necessary materials and assistants for performing sacrifices). He again visited the Naimiṣa forest. The sages, acting as his sacrificial priests, gladly helped Balarāma whose person was constituted of sacrifice (or sacrifices formed a part of his personality) and who kept himself aloof from the (Kaurava-Pāṇḍava) war, in the performance of all sacrifices.

devanagari: तेभ्यो विशुद्धं विज्ञानं भगवान्व्यतरद्विभुः । येनैवात्मन्यदो विश्वमात्मानं विश्वगं विदुः ॥ ३१ ॥
english_devnagari: tebhyo viśuddhaṃ vijñānaṃ bhagavānvyataradvibhuḥyenaivātmanyado viśvamātmānaṃ viśvagaṃ viduḥ
translation: The almighty Lord Balarāma imparted to them highly pure spiritual wisdom by virtue of which the sages realized that the whole universe is in their Self and that their self pervaded all the universe.

devanagari: स्वपत्यावभृथस्नातो ज्ञातिबन्धुसुहृद्वृतः । रेजे स्वज्योत्स्नयेवेन्दुः सुवासाः सुष्ठ्वलङ्कृतः ॥ ३२ ॥
english_devnagari: svapatyāvabhṛthasnāto jñātibandhusuhṛdvṛtaḥreje svajyotsnayevenduḥ suvāsāḥ suṣṭhvalaṅkṛtaḥ
translation: Having performed the concluding bath of the sacrifice along with his consort Ṛevatī and accompanied by his clansmen, relatives and friends, Balarāma who put on excellent dress and was well adorned, shone like the moon radiating its own (moon-) light.

devanagari: ईदृग्विधान्यसङ्ख्यानि बलस्य बलशालिनः । अनन्तस्याप्रमेयस्य मायामर्त्यस्य सन्ति हि ॥ ३३ ॥
english_devnagari: īdṛgvidhānyasaṅkhyāni balasya balaśālinaḥanantasyāprameyasya māyāmartyasya santi hi
translation: Innumerable are other feats and exploits (of such a nature) of the mighty Balarāma who is infinite (or Ananta or the serpent Śeṣa himself) and incomprehensible by nature. He has assumed the form of a mortal being by his own Māyā potency.

devanagari: योऽनुस्मरेत रामस्य कर्माण्यद्भुतकर्मणः । सायं प्रातरनन्तस्य विष्णोः स दयितो भवेत् ॥ ३४ ॥
english_devnagari: yo'nusmareta rāmasya karmāṇyadbhutakarmaṇaḥsāyaṃ prātaranantasya viṣṇoḥ sa dayito bhavet
translation:
He who remembers and contemplates over the feats of Balarāma who performed miraculous deeds, both in the morning and the evening, becomes a beloved of the infinite Lord Viṣṇu.
[back to top]
